source_metadata.title_raw: Krishan Chopra - A-18-254 - December 10, 2018 - Mountain View

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 10, 2018

Krishan Chopra
Senior Assistant City Attorney
City Attorney's Office
500 Castro Street
Mountain View, CA 94041

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-254

Dear Mr. Chopra:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Mountain View City
Councilmember John McAlister regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering
advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are
complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should
change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Councilmember McAlister from taking part in governmental decisions
relating to a development project proposed by a large technology company with multiple office
locations within the City given that one of those offices is a recurring customer of the
Councilmember's Baskin-Robbins ice cream store?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of his interests based
on the facts presented.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-254
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Mountain View and the authorized
representative of City Councilmember John McAlister. The City Council will soon consider
governmental decisions relating to a development project proposed by a large technology company
with multiple existing office locations within the City (the "Company").

The Councilmember is the sole proprietor of a Baskin-Robbins ice cream store located
within the City. The Baskin-Robbins is heavily patronized and sells ice cream to a variety of
customers, including both individuals and businesses. Customers may purchase ice cream or ice
cream related products from the Baskin-Robbins for consumption on site, pickup, or delivery. The
Baskin-Robbins' annual sales revenue is approximately $500,000.

Although the vast majority of the Baskin-Robbins' customers are individual walk-in
customers, the Baskin-Robbins also makes regular, recurring sales to businesses, including
recurring sales to one of the Company's offices located within the City (the "Company Office").
The Baskin-Robbins, however, does not maintain segregated accounts for any sales. The Company
Office orders ice cream from the Baskin-Robbins on an as-needed basis, usually a week in advance
of its need for ice cream, and its orders average approximately $200 per month in cooler months
and $400 per month in warmer months. Based on an estimate of six cool months at $200 per month
and six warm months at $400 per month, the Baskin-Robbins' annual sales to the Company Office
total approximately $3,600, or 0.72 percent of the Baskin-Robbins' annual sales revenue.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests
distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An
official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are
identified in Section 87103. Of those interests, the facts presented indicate that Councilmember
McAlister has the following interests with respect to the decisions at issue:

• A business interest in his Baskin-Robbins, assuming he has an investment worth $2,000 or more
  in that business entity, under Section 87103(a).

• A source of income interest in his Baskin-Robbins under Section 87103(c) because he will
  aggregate $500 or more from it in income in the 12 months prior the decisions at issue.

• An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances pursuant to Section 87103.

Income from Retail Sales

At issue is whether the Councilmember also has a source of income interest in the Company
because his Baskin-Robbins sells the Company Office approximately $3,600 of ice cream per year.
Section 82030 defines "income" for purposes of the Act. Subdivision (a) of that section provides
that "income" means "a payment received," including, but not limited to, the "proceeds of any

File No. A-18-254
Page No. 3

sale." Subdivision (a) also provides that "[i]ncome of an individual" includes "a pro rata share of
any income of any business entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater."

Because the Councilmember is the sole proprietor of the Baskin-Robbins, his ownership
interest in that business exceeds 10 percent, and his income includes all of the Baskin-Robbins'
income. Therefore, unless an exception applies, the Councilmember would also have an interest in
any source of income to the Baskin-Robbins that aggregates $500 or more in income to the
Councilmember in the 12 months prior to the decisions at issue under Section 87103(c).

Section 87103.5(a) sets forth an exception to Section 87103(c)'s definition of an official's
source of income interest for a retail customer of a business engaged in retail sales of goods or
services to the public generally. Whether the exception applies is discussed below.

Do the Retail Customers of the Baskin-Robbins Constitute a "Significant Segment of the Public
Generally"?

Regulation 18702.3(d) provides that the retail customers of a business entity constitute a
significant segment of the public generally for purposes of the exception set forth in Section
87103.5(a) if: (1) the business is open to the public; and (2) the business provides goods or services
to customers that comprise a broad base of persons representative of the jurisdiction as a whole and
not confined to any specialized occupation, profession, or business.

The Baskin-Robbins is located within the City, open to the public, and heavily patronized by
a broad base of ice cream customers. The facts presented provide no indication that the Baskin-
Robbins' broad base of customers is not representative of the City as a whole, or confined to any
specialized occupation, profession, or business. Therefore, the Baskin-Robbins's retail customers
constitute a significant segment of the public generally for purposes of the exception set forth in
Section 87103.5(a) pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(d).

Is the Baskin-Robbins' Income from the Company Indistinguishable from its Income from Other
Retail Customers?

Regulation 18702.3(d) also provides that income from an individual customer is
indistinguishable from the amount of income a business entity receives from other customers "when
the official is unable to recognize a significant monetary difference between the business provided
by the individual customer and the general clientele of the business." Paragraph (1) of that
provision further provides that "[a]n official is unable to recognize a significant monetary difference
when ... [t]he business is of the type that sales to any one customer will not have a significant
impact on the business's annual net sales."

The Baskin-Robbins is unable to provide more than an estimate of its annual ice cream sales
to the Company Office because it does not maintain segregated sales accounts for any of its sales
and because the Company Office only orders ice cream on an as-needed basis. The estimated
annual ice cream sales to the Company Office total approximately 0.72 percent of the Baskin-
Robbins' annual revenue. Because the facts presented indicate that the Baskin-Robbins' sales to any
one customer will not have a significant impact on its annual net sales, the Councilmember is

File No. A-18-254
Page No. 4

unable to recognize a significant monetary difference between the business received from the
Company Office and the Baskin-Robbins' general clientele. Therefore, the Baskin-Robbins' income
from the Company Office is indistinguishable from the amount of income it receives from its other
retail customers for purposes of the exception set forth in Section 87103.5(a).

Thus, the Councilmember does not have a source of income interest in the Company under
Section 87103(c) because the exception set forth in Section 87103.5(a) applies. Accordingly, the
Councilmember has a business interest and a source of income interest in the Baskins-Robbins and
an interest in his personal finances, but does not have a source of income interest in the Company,
with respect to the decisions relating to the development project.

Foreseeability

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability
standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in
the decision, and provides that the effect of the decision on an official's inexplicitly involved
interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than
hypothetical or theoretical."

The facts presented provide no indication that decisions relating to the development project
would have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of the Councilmember's interests.
Therefore, the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By:  Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

locations within the City given that one of those offices is a recurring customer of the 
Councilmember's Baskin-Robbins ice cream store?

No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on any of his interests based 
on the facts presented.

sections.analysis:

in that business entity, under Section 87103(a). 
•

aggregate $500 or more from it in income in the 12 months prior the decisions at issue. 
•

indirectly, or beneficially a IO-percent interest or greater."

Section 87103.5(a) sets forth an exception to Section 87103(c)'s definition of an official's

Do the Retail Customers of the Baskin-Robbins Constitute a "Significant Segment of the Public 
Generally"? 
Regulation 18702.3(d) provides that the retail customers of a business entity constitute a

not confined to any specialized occupation, profession, or business. 
The Baskin-Robbins is located within the City, open to the public, and heavily patronized by 
a broad base of ice cream customers. The facts presented provide no indication that the Baskin-
Robbins' broad base of customers is not representative of the City as a whole, or confined to any 
specialized occupation, profession, or business. Therefore, the Baskin-Robbins's retail customers 
constitute a significant segment of the public generally for purposes of the exception set forth in 
Section 87103.5(a) pursuant to Regulation 18702.3(d). 
Is the Baskin-Robbins' Income from the Company Indistinguishable from its Income from Other 
Retail Customers? 
Regulation 18702.3( d) also provides that income from an individual customer is ' 
indistinguishable from the amount of income a business entity receives from other customers "when 
the official is unable to recognize a significant monetary difference between the business provided 
by the individual customer and the general clientele of the business." Paragraph (1) of that 
provision further provides that "[a]n official is unable to recognize a significant monetary difference 
when ... [t]he business is of the type that sales to any one customer will not have a significant 
impact on the business's annual net sales." 
The Baskin-Robbins is unable to provide more than an estimate of its annual ice cream sales 
to the Company Office because it does not maintain segregated sales accounts for any of its sales 
and because the Company Office only orders ice cream on an as-needed basis. The estimated 
annual ice cream sales to the Company Office total approximately 0.72 percent of the Baskin-
Robbins' annual revenue. Because the facts presented indicate that the Baskin-Robbins' sales to any 
one customer will not have a significant impact on its annual net sales, the Councilmember is  
unable to recognize a significant monetary difference between the business received from the 
Company Office and the Baskin-Robbins' general clientele. Therefore, the Baskin-Robbins' income 
from the Company Office is indistinguishable from the amount of income it receives from its other 
retail customers for purposes of the exception set forth in Section 87103.S(a). 
Thus, the Councilmember does not have a source of income interest in the Company under

Councilmember has a business interest and a source of income interest in the Baskins-Robbins and

with respect to the decisions relating to the development project.
